slug: 种族隔离在美国合法到_1964_年_没那么久以前
title: 种族隔离在美国合法到 1964 年——没那么久以前

outline.concept: 1964 is within one generation — your parents or grandparents likely lived in legally segregated America. This isn't ancient history; it's family memory.
outline.query: 父母 祖父母 成长 美国 segregation 亲身经历

outline.concept: Segregation wasn't social custom — it was state law. Governments criminalized integrated schools, buses, restaurants. Breaking these laws meant arrest, not just social disapproval.
outline.query: 法律 强制 分离 学校 巴士 餐厅 Jim Crow arrest

outline.concept: The Supreme Court's 'separate but equal' doctrine (Plessy v Ferguson, 1896) gave constitutional cover: the legal fiction that segregated facilities could be equal, when they systematically, deliberately weren't.
outline.query: separate but equal 最高法院 Plessy 宪法 合法 掩饰 不平等

outline.concept: Brown v Board (1954) declared school segregation unconstitutional, but court rulings alone lack enforcement teeth. Real desegregation required the 1964 Civil Rights Act's threat to cut federal funding — money talks louder than precedent.
outline.query: Brown v Board 1954 执行 Civil Rights Act 1964 联邦 资金 威胁

outline.concept: People who lived under legal segregation are still alive. The wealth gap, housing patterns, and institutional effects from state-mandated segregation persist across generations — the law changed; the accumulated damage didn't reset.
outline.query: 亲历者 在世 代际 财富差距 housing segregation 持续影响
